mnemonics.title: AES Key-Round Association
mnemonics.content: JERKS: 128 = 10 rounds, 192 = 12 rounds, 256 = 14 rounds. Think: 'JERKS get worse with more power' - more key bits = more rounds.

mnemonics.title: ECB Problem Rememberer
mnemonics.content: ECB = Electronic Codebook = Electronic 'Can't Be Secure' - identical inputs give identical outputs, revealing patterns.

mnemonics.title: Block Cipher Modes
mnemonics.content: CBC: Chain blocks together; CTR: Count up; GCM: Galois/Counter + Authentication; ECB: Each block alone.

acronyms.usage: The current NIST standard for symmetric encryption, superseding DES.
Legacy symmetric cipher, now considered insecure due to small key size.
Enhanced DES applying cipher three times for better security.
Block cipher mode that XORs each block with previous ciphertext.
Block cipher mode using counter values for encryption.
Authenticated encryption mode providing confidentiality and integrity.
Simplest but insecure block cipher mode; encrypts blocks independently.
Random value ensuring unique ciphertext for same plaintext.
Cipher structure combining S-boxes and P-boxes for confusion and diffusion.
Lookup table providing non-linear transformation for confusion.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine dominoes in a row. Each ciphertext block 'knocks over' the next block. The first block needs an IV 'push' to start the chain.
Think of a single snowflake triggering an avalanche. One input bit change causes approximately half of output bits to flip, like a small event causing a massive cascade.
S-box (Substitution) = shuffling cards randomly (confusion); P-box (Permutation) = rearranging positions (diffusion). Together they make patterns unrecognizable.
Imagine Alice and Bob need matching door keys but can only communicate through a mailman they don't trust. This is the key distribution challenge in symmetric encryption.

analogies.analogy: Like a safe deposit box where both Alice and Bob have the same key. It works great once both have the key, but how do they exchange the key safely?
Like color-coding documents. If all 'CONFIDENTIAL' documents are stamped with the same red stamp, anyone seeing the stamps knows they're confidential - patterns are visible.
Like using a different nonce (unique number) each time you lock your door with the same key. Even if someone has your key, each lock combination is different, so they can't predict the next one.
Like writing pages in a book: ECB writes each page separately (predictable); CBC chains pages together (secure); CTR numbers pages sequentially (fast, random access).
